fact: The first official Test cricket match was played in 1877 between Australia and England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground.
Brian Lara holds the record for the highest individual score in Test cricket, scoring 400 not out against England in 2004.
The ICC Cricket World Cup was first held in 1975 in England, with the West Indies winning the inaugural tournament.
Sachin Tendulkar is the only cricketer to have scored 100 international centuries across Test and One Day International formats.
In cricket, a bowler is credited with a hat-trick when they dismiss three batsmen with three consecutive deliveries.
Lord's Cricket Ground in London, home to Marylebone Cricket Club, is often referred to as the home of cricket.
Muttiah Muralitharan holds the record for most wickets in Test cricket, taking 800 wickets during his career for Sri Lanka.
The Indian Premier League, launched in 2008, is a Twenty20 franchise cricket league organized by the Board of Control for Cricket in India.
Australia has won the ICC Cricket World Cup a record six times, including titles in 1987, 1999, 2003, 2007, 2015, and 2023.
The term 'googly' refers to a deceptive delivery bowled by a leg-spinner that turns unexpectedly in the opposite direction.
Sir Donald Bradman finished his Test career with a batting average of 99.94, widely regarded as the greatest statistic in sport.
The first Cricket World Cup for women was held in 1973, two years before the men's edition, and was won by England.
The Decision Review System, introduced in 2008, allows teams to challenge on-field umpiring decisions using technology like Hawk-Eye.
The Melbourne Cricket Ground, opened in 1853, has a capacity of over 100,000 spectators, making it one of the largest stadiums in the world.
Rohit Sharma holds the record for the highest individual score in a One Day International, scoring 264 runs against Sri Lanka in 2014.
The first official football World Cup was held in Uruguay in 1930, with the host nation winning the inaugural title.
Cristiano Ronaldo is the all-time top scorer in men's international football, surpassing 130 goals for Portugal.
Lionel Messi has won the Ballon d'Or award a record eight times during his career with Barcelona, Paris Saint-Germain, and Inter Miami.
The UEFA Champions League is Europe's premier club football competition, with Real Madrid winning the title a record 15 times.
A football match consists of two 45-minute halves, with additional stoppage time added by the referee for delays during play.
Camp Nou in Barcelona, home to FC Barcelona, is one of the largest football stadiums in Europe with a capacity exceeding 99,000.
Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup a record five times, in 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.
The offside rule prevents an attacking player from being in an advantageous position closer to the goal line than both the ball and the second-last defender.
Josef Bican is credited by FIFA with scoring over 800 career goals, making him one of the most prolific goalscorers in football history.
The English Football Association, founded in 1863, was the first governing body established to codify the rules of association football.
The UEFA European Championship, first held in 1960, is contested every four years among Europe's national football teams.
Pele won three FIFA World Cup titles with Brazil in 1958, 1962, and 1970, a feat unmatched by any other player.
A hat-trick in football refers to a single player scoring three goals in one match.
The Maracana Stadium in Rio de Janeiro once held over 199,000 spectators for the 1950 World Cup final between Brazil and Uruguay.
Lionel Messi holds the record for most goals scored in a single calendar year, netting 91 goals in 2012 for club and country.
The Wimbledon Championships, first held in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and is played on grass courts.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for most Grand Slam men's singles titles, having won 24 major championships in his career.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, the most by any player in the Open Era of tennis.
The four Grand Slam tournaments in tennis are the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and the US Open.
A tennis set is typically won by the first player to reach six games with a margin of at least two games over their opponent.
The French Open is played at Stade Roland Garros in Paris on distinctive red clay courts, unlike the other three Grand Slams.
In tennis scoring, the term 'love' is used to denote a score of zero points in a game.
Margaret Court holds the record for most Grand Slam singles titles overall, with 24 wins spanning the amateur and Open Eras.
Roger Federer held the record for most weeks ranked world number one at the time of his retirement in 2022.
The Open Era of tennis began in 1968 when Grand Slam tournaments allowed both amateur and professional players to compete.
The US Open was the first Grand Slam tournament to introduce a tie-break system to decide sets, starting in 1970.
The Davis Cup, first contested in 1900, is the premier international team competition in men's tennis, organized by country.
Modern badminton originated in British India in the 19th century before being formalized in England during the 1870s.
A standard badminton match is played as the best of three games, with each game won by the first player to reach 21 points.
Lin Dan is one of only two male players to complete a career Grand Slam, winning all major badminton titles including Olympic gold twice.
The All England Open Badminton Championships, first held in 1899, is the oldest and most prestigious badminton tournament in the world.
The fastest recorded badminton smash exceeded 565 kilometers per hour, achieved by Malaysian player Tan Boon Heong in testing conditions.
Indonesia has won the Thomas Cup, badminton's premier men's team event, more times than any other nation since the tournament began in 1949.
The Badminton World Federation is headquartered in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, overseeing global governance of the sport.
A badminton shuttlecock, or birdie, is traditionally made from 16 goose feathers attached to a rounded cork base.
P.V. Sindhu became the first Indian woman to win an Olympic silver medal in badminton at the 2016 Rio Olympics.
China's badminton team has historically dominated the Uber Cup, the women's team world championship first held in 1956-57.
Badminton became an official Olympic sport at the 1992 Barcelona Games, after appearing as a demonstration sport in earlier editions.
In badminton, a let is called when play is stopped and restarted without a change in score due to an unforeseen disturbance.
Basketball was invented in 1891 by Canadian physical education instructor James Naismith at a YMCA training school in Massachusetts.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar was the all-time leading scorer in NBA history for nearly four decades before LeBron James surpassed his record in 2023.
Michael Jordan won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls and was named Finals MVP in each of those title runs.
The NBA Finals determine the champion of the National Basketball Association, played as a best-of-seven series between conference champions.
A standard NBA basketball game consists of four quarters, each lasting 12 minutes, for a total of 48 minutes of regulation play.
The Boston Celtics and Los Angeles Lakers share the record for most NBA championships won, each with 17 titles as of 2023.
Madison Square Garden in New York City, home to the New York Knicks, is one of the most iconic arenas in basketball history.
The three-point line was introduced to the NBA in the 1979-80 season, changing offensive strategies across the league.
Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points in a single NBA game in 1962, a record that has never been matched or broken.
LeBron James has won NBA championships with three different franchises: the Miami Heat, Cleveland Cavaliers, and Los Angeles Lakers.
The NBA was founded in 1946 as the Basketball Association of America before merging with the National Basketball League in 1949.
A basketball hoop is regulated to stand exactly 10 feet, or 3.05 meters, above the playing surface in professional competition.
Field hockey has ancient origins, with stick-and-ball games traced back to civilizations in Egypt, Persia, and Greece thousands of years ago.
India won the Olympic field hockey gold medal a record eight times, dominating the sport from 1928 through 1980.
A standard field hockey match is played over four quarters of 15 minutes each, following rule changes introduced by the FIH in 2014.
The Hockey World Cup, first held in 1971, is organized by the International Hockey Federation and features top national teams globally.
Dhyan Chand, an Indian field hockey player, scored over 400 goals in his career and helped India win three Olympic gold medals.
The Kalinga Stadium in Bhubaneswar, India, has hosted multiple editions of the Hockey World Cup, including in 2018 and 2023.
In field hockey, players are prohibited from using the rounded back side of the stick to hit or control the ball.
The Netherlands men's and women's field hockey teams are both among the most successful nations in modern World Cup and Olympic competition.
A penalty corner in field hockey is awarded for defensive infractions inside the circle, giving the attacking team a set-piece scoring opportunity.
Field hockey was introduced as an Olympic sport for men in 1908 at the London Games, though it was later dropped and reinstated.
The first Formula 1 World Championship race was held at Silverstone Circuit in England on May 13, 1950.
Lewis Hamilton and Michael Schumacher share the record for most Formula 1 World Drivers' Championships, each having won seven titles.
Michael Schumacher won five consecutive Formula 1 World Championships with Ferrari between 2000 and 2004.
The Monaco Grand Prix, held on the narrow streets of Monte Carlo, is regarded as one of the most prestigious races on the F1 calendar.
In Formula 1, drivers are awarded points based on their finishing position, with the race winner earning 25 points under current regulations.
Scuderia Ferrari is the oldest surviving team in Formula 1, having competed continuously since the championship began in 1950.
The Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps in Belgium is known for its challenging Eau Rouge corner and unpredictable weather conditions.
A Formula 1 pit stop for tire changes can be completed by a skilled crew in under three seconds during competitive races.
Sebastian Vettel became the youngest Formula 1 World Champion in 2010 at age 23, driving for Red Bull Racing.
Ayrton Senna won three Formula 1 World Championships and is widely remembered as one of the sport's most skilled and daring drivers.
The Constructors' Championship in Formula 1 was introduced in 1958, eight years after the Drivers' Championship began.
The safety car is deployed in Formula 1 to neutralize dangerous track conditions while allowing the race to continue at reduced speed.
The modern Olympic Games were revived in 1896 in Athens, Greece, through the efforts of French educator Pierre de Coubertin.
Michael Phelps holds the record for most Olympic medals won by an individual, with 28 medals across four Olympic Games.
Usain Bolt won eight Olympic gold medals in sprinting events, including three consecutive 100-meter titles from 2008 to 2016.
The Summer and Winter Olympic Games are each held every four years, staggered two years apart since 1994.
The Bird's Nest Stadium in Beijing hosted the opening and closing ceremonies for the 2008 Summer Olympics.
The five interlocking rings of the Olympic symbol represent the union of five inhabited continents participating in the Games.
The 1904 St. Louis Olympics were the first Games held outside Europe, coinciding with the Louisiana Purchase Exposition.
Larisa Latynina, a Soviet gymnast, held the record for most Olympic medals until Michael Phelps surpassed her total in 2012.
The United States has won more total Olympic medals than any other nation across the history of the modern Summer Games.
The Olympic flame is lit months before each Games in Olympia, Greece, and relayed by torchbearers to the host city.
Nadia Comaneci became the first gymnast to score a perfect 10 in Olympic competition at the 1976 Montreal Games.
The Panathenaic Stadium in Athens, built entirely of marble, hosted the first modern Olympic Games in 1896.
